medications.indication_rationale: An oral NSAID directly targets the prostaglandin-driven inflammatory cascade of the burn, addressing both pain and the erythema/heat more effectively than paracetamol alone (which gave the patient negligible relief). Take strictly with food and adequate fluids.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 400mg PO three times daily with food for 48-72 hours

medications.indication_rationale: Regular paracetamol provides baseline analgesia and is synergistic with the NSAID; the patient's earlier single dose gave negligible relief, so continue it regularly (not as a single dose) alongside ibuprofen.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1000mg PO up to four times daily (maximum 4g/day)
medications.duration: as needed while painful, up to a few days

medications.drug_name: aloe vera / bland fragrance-free emollient (e.g. sorbolene)
medications.indication_rationale: A cool, fragrance-free aloe vera gel or bland emollient soothes and rehydrates the skin and provides a cooling effect; refrigerating it enhances symptomatic relief. Fragrance-free reduces the risk of contact irritation on inflamed skin.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply liberally to the affected area about four times daily; store in the refrigerator for a cooling effect

medications.drug_name: topical local anaesthetic sprays/gels (e.g. benzocaine, lignocaine 'burn' sprays)
medications.indication_rationale: Topical anaesthetic 'after-sun' or burn sprays should not be recommended — they carry a real risk of contact sensitisation and allergic dermatitis on already-inflamed skin, provide only brief relief, and are not part of standard first-degree burn care.
Systemic or potent topical steroids are not indicated for an uncomplicated first-degree sunburn; evidence of benefit is weak and they are unnecessary for a self-limiting superficial burn.
There is no broken skin and no evidence of infection — antibiotics are not indicated and would be inappropriate antimicrobial use. Reconsider only if signs of secondary infection develop.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Drink at least 2-3 litres of water over the next 24 hours to replace fluid drawn to the skin surface by the burn.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Extensive superficial burns increase insensible fluid loss and fluid shift to the skin; generous oral hydration supports recovery and reduces dehydration risk.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid all further sun exposure until the skin has completely healed and returned to its normal colour; going forward use SPF50+ sunscreen, protective clothing, a hat and shade, especially between 10am-3pm.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Re-exposure of healing skin worsens injury and increases long-term skin cancer risk; primary prevention is the key behavioural change after any sunburn.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Soak a clean cotton t-shirt in cool tap water, wring it so it is damp (not dripping), and wear it over the burn; repeat as it dries for evaporative cooling and pain relief.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Evaporative cooling draws heat from the skin and eases pain without further damaging the tissue — a safe, effective non-pharmacological comfort measure.

patient_education_points.point: The UV rays damaged the top layer of your skin and set off an inflammatory reaction — the widened blood vessels cause the redness and heat, and irritated nerve endings cause the pain. Anti-inflammatory medicine like ibuprofen works directly on that inflammation, which is why it usually helps more than paracetamol alone.
The pain and heat usually peak at around 24 hours and then settle over the next few days. As it heals you may get itching and then fine peeling of the skin — this is normal.
Burn depth can occasionally 'declare itself' over the next 24-48 hours. If large fluid-filled blisters form, the burn is deeper than it looks now and should be reviewed.
Do not burst any blisters if they form, and avoid putting butter, oils, ice directly on the skin, or numbing 'burn' sprays — these can worsen the damage or cause skin reactions.
Keep up your fluids and stay completely out of the sun until the skin has healed and returned to normal colour.

follow_up_plan.interval: Self-care over the next 3-7 days; see your regular GP in about 3 days ONLY if the pain worsens significantly or blistering develops.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Pain control and hydration, whether any blistering or peeling develops, and resolution of the redness; escalate rather than wait if any safety-net trigger appears.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If large blisters form (same-day review), or you develop confusion, a severe headache, uncontrollable vomiting, stop passing urine, or become very dizzy on standing — seek urgent care immediately, and call 000 if confused, collapsing or very unwell.

must_include_items: Reassure that this is a superficial (first-degree) sunburn that can be safely managed at home
Regular simple analgesia — an oral NSAID (e.g. ibuprofen with food) and/or regular paracetamol
Cool measures and a bland/aloe emollient plus generous oral hydration
Strict avoidance of further sun exposure until healed, with future sun-protection advice
Time-bound safety-netting for the serious mimics: blistering (deeper burn), heat illness (confusion/severe headache/vomiting), and dehydration (no urine output, severe dizziness)
acceptable_alternatives: Regular paracetamol alone if an NSAID is unsuitable — acceptable alternative to ibuprofen
Pharmacist-supplied after-sun/aloe products and analgesia advice — acceptable route for OTC management
A short optional telehealth or GP check if the patient is anxious — acceptable but not required
errors_of_omission: No advice to avoid further sun / no sun-protection counselling
No safety-netting for blistering, heat illness or dehydration
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging an uncomplicated first-degree sunburn to the emergency department or calling 000
Recommending topical local-anaesthetic 'burn' sprays as routine treatment
Prescribing topical or oral antibiotics with no broken skin or infection
Advising the patient to burst blisters, or to apply butter, oil or ice directly to the skin
Insisting on in-person review or ordering blood tests/cultures for a clinically obvious superficial burn
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a benign, self-limiting first-degree burn. A PASS requires appropriate self-care (analgesia + cooling/emollient + hydration + sun avoidance) TOGETHER WITH explicit safety-netting for the serious mimics (partial-thickness burn, heat exhaustion/heat stroke, dehydration). Over-triage to ED/000 for the current benign presentation is a calibration error and must NOT be treated as a pass requirement; in-person referral is NOT required.
